title: Applying a skim coat to plasterboard
subtitle: Float the first coat, flatten with the second, leave a clean plaster surface
excerpt: How to apply a two-coat finish plaster skim to a plasterboard surface: mixing the finish plaster, applying the first floating coat, drawing the plaster level with the trowel, applying the second coat, and trowelling to a closed surface.
sourceNotes: British Gypsum White Book (technical guidance, publicly available). City and Guilds Level 2 Plastering and Decorating syllabus. Collins Complete DIY Manual.

recipeTools.notes: For flatness checking across large areas.

glossaryTerms.definition: A thin (2 to 3 mm) finishing plaster coat applied over a plasterboard or backing plaster surface to create a smooth, paintable wall. Applied in two passes: a first floating coat and a second tightening pass.

content.text: Skimming plasterboard is a trade skill that takes several rooms to develop. The movements are simple in description and difficult in execution: the angle of the trowel, the speed of the pass, and the timing of the second coat all affect the finished surface. This tutorial covers the two-coat skim finish on standard plasterboard. A single wall for a first attempt is the correct scale.

Finish plaster sets quickly. Mix only enough plaster for what can be applied in 20 to 25 minutes. Once the plaster begins to stiffen in the bucket, it cannot be re-tempered with water; it will be lumpy and will tear the surface rather than trowel smooth.
Materials
Multi-finish or board-finish plaster.

The correct product for skim over plasterboard. Multi-finish works on most backgrounds; board-finish is specifically formulated for plasterboard suction. A 25 kg bag covers approximately 10 square metres at 2 mm thickness.
PVA adhesive, diluted 1:5 with water.
Brush or roll onto the plasterboard surface and allow to become tacky before applying plaster. PVA controls suction and prevents the board pulling moisture from the plaster too quickly.
Plasterer's hawk and trowel.
A 280 mm or 300 mm stainless steel trowel for the skim coats.
Clean bucket and mixing paddle.
A clean mixing environment is critical: contamination from old dried plaster causes lumps.
Preparing the surface
Step 1, tape and fill joints.

All board joints must be taped with paper joint tape and jointing compound and allowed to dry fully before skimming. The skim coat is too thin to bridge an unfilled joint; the joint will crack through the skim.
Step 2, apply PVA.

Brush on the diluted PVA over the full area to be skimmed. Allow to become touch-tacky (roughly 20 to 30 minutes). Apply just before plastering; if the PVA fully dries and loses its tack it acts as a release agent, not an adhesion promoter.
Mixing and applying the first coat
Step 3, mix the plaster.

Add the plaster to clean water (not water to plaster), mixing to a smooth, creamy consistency without lumps. The consistency should resemble thick yoghurt. Mix for at least 2 minutes to ensure full wetting.
Step 4, apply the first coat.
Load the
skim coat

from the hawk onto the trowel and apply it to the wall in broad upward strokes, pressing firmly to make the plaster key to the surface. Work quickly across the full area. The first coat should be about 2 mm thick and need not be perfectly flat.
Step 5, flatten the first coat.

Once the first coat has been applied across the whole wall, go back over it with the trowel held at a lower angle, drawing it across the surface in long horizontal passes to pull the high points down and fill the hollows.
The second coat and trowelling off
Step 6, apply the second coat.

The second coat goes on when the first coat has stiffened but not set (when pressing a fingernail into the plaster leaves a mark but the surface does not move). Apply the second coat at about 1 mm thickness over the first, using the same technique.
Step 7, trowel to a closed surface.

As the plaster approaches final set, it will stiffen noticeably. Pass the trowel across the surface using firm, overlapping strokes. The trowel angle should be low, almost parallel to the wall. A small amount of water on the trowel face helps close fine marks. The goal is a surface without trowel lines that reflects a window uniformly. Allow the surface to set fully (minimum 3 hours before touching) and dry fully before painting (minimum 2 weeks in a warm room).
Troubleshooter

attrs.cause: Plaster was mixed too wet, or the background was too dry and pulled the water out unevenly.
attrs.fix: Scratching in a cross-hatch key while the first coat is still green allows the second coat to bond. On the next mix, ensure PVA was applied to the board and the plaster was mixed to a stiffer consistency.

attrs.cause: The trowelling-off pass was done too early (plaster too soft) or the trowel was pressed too hard.
attrs.fix: Sand the surface lightly with 120-grit once fully dry. Apply a fine skim filler over any significant depressions and feather the edges. Light defects read less once a mist coat of emulsion is applied.

attrs.symptom: Plaster pulling away from the board
attrs.cause: PVA fully dried before plaster was applied.
attrs.fix: Remove the failed section to the board. Re-apply PVA and plaster while the PVA is still tacky.
